items.question_text: Under 5-5-1b3, radar separation may be applied between a radar-identified aircraft and one not radar-identified when either is cleared to climb/descend through the altitude of the other. Which of the following is NOT one of the required conditions?
items.explanation: The five conditions are: (a) adequate radar system performance with primary/ASR-9 targets displayed; (b) flight data indicates the non-identified aircraft can give adequate return; (c) airspace is not less than the specified miles from the edge of the display; (d) radar separation is maintained between the identified aircraft and all observed targets until nonradar separation is established; (e) when on the same relative heading, the identified aircraft is vectored sufficiently to avoid superimposed targets. 'The non-identified aircraft must squawk a discrete beacon code' is not a condition.

choices.text: The performance of the radar system is adequate and primary/ASR-9 targets are displayed.
Flight data indicates the non-identified aircraft can give adequate primary radar return.
The non-identified aircraft must squawk a discrete beacon code.
When on the same relative heading, the identified aircraft is vectored sufficiently to avoid superimposed targets.

items.question_text: Under 5-5-1b3c, what are the minimum distances from the edge of the radar display when applying radar separation between identified and non-identified aircraft?
items.explanation: The distances are: less than 40 miles from antenna = 6 miles; 40 miles or more = 10 miles; narrowband radar = 10 miles.

choices.text: 6 miles (<40 NM), 10 miles (>=40 NM), 10 miles (narrowband).
3 miles (<40 NM), 5 miles (>=40 NM), 5 miles (narrowband).
1.5 miles (<40 NM), 2.5 miles (>=40 NM), 5 miles (narrowband).
5 miles regardless of distance from antenna.

items.question_text: Under 5-5-2a, radar separation is applied between the centers of primary radar targets. What must NOT be allowed?
items.explanation: 5-5-2a states: do not allow a primary target to touch another primary target or a beacon control slash.

choices.text: A primary target to touch another primary target or a beacon control slash.
Beacon control slashes to touch data blocks.
Digital targets to appear without altitude readouts.
Two beacon targets to share the same discrete code.

items.question_text: NOTE: Target resolution (5-5-3) must NOT be provided utilizing what type of radar system?
items.explanation: The note under 5-5-3 states this procedure must not be provided utilizing mosaic radar systems.

choices.text: mosaic radar systems

items.question_text: Under 5-5-4b FUSION, what is the normal separation minimum for a Fusion target symbol?
items.explanation: 5-5-4b1: Fusion target symbol = 3 miles. 5-5-4b2: when ISR is displayed = 5 miles.

choices.text: 3 miles.
5 miles.
2.5 miles.
10 miles.

items.question_text: Under 5-5-4, what ERAM separation applies up to and including FL230 when all 3 NM conditions are met?
items.explanation: 5-5-4d3: ERAM may use 3 miles up to and including FL230 when within the 3 NM separation area and all conditions are met (within 40 NM of preferred radar, or 60 NM with MSSR, or track-based display mode; reliable targets; facility directive defining the area; area displayable on video map; 3 NM target symbol displayed).

choices.text: 3 miles.
5 miles.
10 miles.
2.5 miles.

items.question_text: Under 5-5-4 TERMINAL, what separation is required when NOWGT is displayed in an aircraft's data block?
items.explanation: 5-5-4i: when NOWGT is displayed, provide 10 miles separation behind the preceding aircraft and 10 miles separation to the succeeding aircraft.

choices.text: 10 miles behind the preceding aircraft and 10 miles to the succeeding aircraft.
5 miles behind and 5 miles to the succeeding aircraft.
3 miles in all directions.
Standard wake turbulence minima based on weight category.

items.question_text: Under 5-5-4f1a en route wake turbulence: behind a super aircraft, the separation is 5 miles UNLESS the super is operating at or below what altitude and below what speed?
items.explanation: 5-5-4f1a: Behind super = 5 miles, UNLESS the super is at or below FL240 and below 250 knots, then: Heavy = 6 miles, Large = 7 miles, Small = 8 miles.

choices.text: At or below FL240 and below 250 knots.
At or below FL180 and below 200 knots.
At or below FL310 and below 280 knots.
At or below FL100 and below 150 knots.

items.question_text: Under 5-5-4j TERMINAL, what reduced separation is authorized and under what conditions?
items.explanation: 5-5-4j: 2.5 NM separation is authorized between aircraft established on final approach course within 10 NM of the landing runway when operating in FUSION or single sensor slant range mode (if within 40 miles of antenna), with wake turbulence applied per TBL 5-5-2, average runway occupancy time of 50 seconds or less documented, CTRDs operational, and turnoff points visible from the tower.

choices.text: 2.5 NM, when established on final within 10 NM of the runway in FUSION or single sensor slant range, with four additional conditions met.
1.5 NM, available at all towered airports within 40 miles of the antenna.
2.5 NM, available at all en route facilities up to FL600.
3 NM, reduced from 5 NM when two consecutive CTRDs show the runway clear.

items.question_text: Under 5-5-10b, separate radar-controlled aircraft from the boundary of airspace using nonradar separation. What is the minimum when less than 40 miles from the antenna?
items.explanation: 5-5-10b1: when less than 40 miles from the antenna, use 3 miles from the boundary when the adjacent airspace uses nonradar separation.

choices.text: 3 miles

items.question_text: Under 5-5-10c, for VFR aircraft receiving Class B, Class C, or TRSA services near adjacent airspace, what is the boundary protection requirement?
items.explanation: 5-5-10c: the provisions of a and b do not apply; instead, ensure targets of these aircraft do not touch the boundary of adjacent airspace.

choices.text: Targets must not touch the boundary of adjacent airspace.
The same boundary minima as IFR aircraft apply.
No boundary protection is required for VFR aircraft.
Keep VFR aircraft 10 miles from every boundary.

items.question_text: Under 5-5-11 EAS, what minimum applies from the edge of scope when below Flight Level 600?
items.explanation: 5-5-11c1: EAS below FL600 = 5 miles from edge of scope.

choices.text: 5 miles

items.question_text: Under 5-5-12, when using a radar target display with previously specified beacon target displacement, what correction factor is added to the applicable minima?
items.explanation: The correction factor is 1 mile. The maximum allowable beacon target displacement that may be specified is 1/2 mile.

choices.text: 1 mile

items.situation: You are working an aircraft that has just departed and will be radar identified within 1 mile of the runway end. A second radar-identified aircraft is on approach to a crossing runway. You need to apply separation between them.
items.question_text: Under 5-5-1b2, may you apply radar separation between the departing aircraft and the radar-identified arrival?

choices.text: Yes, radar separation may be applied because the departure will be radar-identified within 1 mile of the runway end.
No, radar separation may be applied only between two already-identified aircraft.
No, departure and arrival separation always requires nonradar procedures.
Yes, but only if the departure is given a heading within 30 seconds of takeoff.

items.explanation: 5-5-1B2 explicitly allows radar separation between an aircraft taking off and another radar-identified aircraft when the departure will be radar-identified within 1 mile of the runway end.

items.situation: Terminal single sensor ASR or DTAS, less than 40 miles from antenna.
items.question_text: What is the lateral separation minimum?

choices.text: 3 miles.
5 miles.
2.5 miles.
1.5 miles.

items.explanation: 5-5-4A1: Single Sensor ASR/DTAS uses 3 miles when less than 40 miles from the antenna.

items.situation: You are transitioning an aircraft from terminal to en route control. The two aircraft are on diverging courses and separation is constantly increasing. An LOA between the facilities covers the procedure on this route. You currently have 3 miles and it is increasing.
items.question_text: Under 5-5-4d4, may you transfer control with 3 miles increasing to 5 miles or greater?

choices.text: Yes, when the procedure is covered by an LOA, the aircraft are on diverging routes and/or the leader is faster, separation is constantly increasing, and the first center controller will establish 5 NM before the aircraft leaves the first center sector.
No, you must have 5 miles before initiating any handoff to a center.
Yes, separation only needs to be constant, not increasing.
No, the terminal-to-en-route transition always requires 10 miles regardless of the LOA.

items.explanation: 5-5-4D4 permits 3 miles increasing to 5 miles or greater during terminal-to-en-route transition when all four conditions are met: diverging routes and/or leader faster, separation constantly increasing, first center controller establishes 5 NM, and LOA covers the procedure.
